9.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. MLX handles all tensor math.

10.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

11.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset into a random training order.

13.text: mlx.core is MLX's tensor library, similar to NumPy but runs on Apple Silicon GPU. Arrays live in unified memory shared between CPU and GPU.

14.text: mlx.nn provides neural network modules, feels like PyTorch's nn module.

15.text: MLX ships with built-in optimizers like Adam, so we don't need to implement the update logic manually as we did in JAX.

16.text: Utility functions for working with MLX's parameter trees.

19.text: MLX has its own RNG, seeded separately.

24.text: Dataset path, shared across all labs.

25.text: If the names dataset is not already on disk, download it automatically so the tutorial is self-contained.

28.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

31.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings. Each line becomes one training document.

32.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

35.text: The set of unique characters across all names forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID.

36.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

37.text: The vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

44.text: Split attention into 4 parallel heads so each can focus on different character relationships.

45.text: A single transformer layer keeps things minimal. One layer is enough to learn meaningful character-level patterns for names.

46.text: Names up to 16 characters fit in the context window. Most names are well within this limit.

47.text: Each attention head works with 4 dimensions, matching the small model scale.

50.text: RMSNorm module. MLX uses __call__ instead of PyTorch's forward, Python convention for callable objects.

53.text: A tiny constant added before division to prevent numerical explosion when activations are near zero. Standard in every norm layer.

55.text: MLX uses __call__ for the forward pass, not forward. A stylistic choice, both work.

56.text: Element-wise square, then mean. MLX arrays support the same operations as NumPy.

57.text: Scale by inverse RMS. mx.rsqrt is a fused reciprocal square root.

60.text: Self-attention module, structurally identical to PyTorch's version in lab 03.

63.text: nn.Linear in MLX works just like PyTorch's, stores a weight matrix, applies matrix multiply.

64.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

65.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry content to be aggregated by attention.

66.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

68.text: Forward pass. No batch dimension here, MLX's single-example version operates on (T, C) tensors.

69.text: Extract the sequence length from the input tensor to set up attention masks.

70.text: Reshape into heads: (T, D) -> (T, nh, hd) -> (nh, T, hd). Same pattern across every lab.

71.text: Keys get the same multi-head reshape as queries. Each head's keys will be compared against only that head's queries.

72.text: Values get the same multi-head reshape. Each head independently retrieves content from its own subspace of the embedding.

74.text: Compute attention scores by taking the dot product of queries and keys, scaled by the square root of head dimension to prevent saturation.

75.text: Causal mask. MLX's triu works identically to NumPy/PyTorch.

76.text: Apply the causal mask using element-wise selection. Future positions get a large negative value so softmax assigns them zero weight.

77.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

79.text: Attention output: weighted sum, transpose back, reshape to (T, D).

80.text: Project the combined multi-head output through the final linear layer.

86.text: Expand the representation to 4x the embedding dimension. This wider hidden layer gives the MLP more capacity for nonlinear transformations.

87.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

89.text: Run the input through the MLP sub-layer combining expansion, activation, and contraction.

90.text: mx.maximum(x, 0) is MLX's ReLU. No separate F.relu function, just use the element-wise maximum.

93.text: Transformer block, same norm-attend-add, norm-MLP-add pattern.

96.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

97.text: The self-attention sub-layer that mixes information across positions in the sequence.

98.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

99.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

101.text: Forward: same two-line transformer block as PyTorch.

102.text: Apply attention with a residual connection: normalize, attend to other positions, and add the result back to the original signal.

103.text: Apply the MLP with a residual connection that preserves the original signal.

107.text: The complete GPT model combining embeddings, normalization, transformer layers, and output head.

110.text: The token embedding lookup table. Each of the 27 tokens maps to a learned 16-dimensional vector capturing what that character 'means' to the model.

111.text: The position embedding table. Each of the 16 positions maps to a learned vector that tells the model where a token sits in the sequence.

112.text: Normalize embeddings before entering the transformer stack. This initial normalization keeps activations stable from the very start.

113.text: Transformer layers. MLX uses a plain Python list (not ModuleList), the framework still discovers parameters through its tree-walking utilities.

114.text: The output head projects from 16-dim embedding space to 27-dim vocabulary logits. Each output score indicates how likely a character is to come next.

116.text: Run the full model on the current input to produce next-token predictions.

117.text: Extract the sequence length from the input to generate matching position indices.

118.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token in the sequence.

119.text: Generate position indices [0, 1, ..., n-1] and look up their learned embeddings. These give the model a sense of token order.

120.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

121.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. The code scales to any depth even though we use just one layer here.

122.text: Each layer transforms the hidden state.

123.text: Map from embedding space to vocabulary logits. Each output value is a raw score for how likely each of the 27 tokens is to come next.

129.text: Instantiate the model. No .to(device) needed, MLX's unified memory means arrays are already accessible by both CPU and GPU.

131.text: Flatten the parameter tree for re-initialization.

132.text: Replace all weights with N(0, 0.08) random values. MLX doesn't have PyTorch's apply method, so we reinitialize through load_weights.

133.text: Sum up all parameter sizes to report the total number of trainable weights.

136.text: Adam hyperparameters, same across all labs.

139.text: Loss function takes the MODEL as its first argument, this is how MLX's value_and_grad knows which parameters to differentiate.

140.text: Run the full model on the current input to get next-token prediction logits.

142.text: Manual log-softmax: logits - logsumexp(logits). More numerically stable than log(softmax(x)).

143.text: Get the number of tokens in the input for indexing into log probabilities.

144.text: Gather target log-probs and negate their mean, cross-entropy loss.

148.text: nn.value_and_grad creates a function that returns both the loss value and gradients in one call. MLX's equivalent of PyTorch's forward + backward.

149.text: MLX's built-in Adam optimizer. Unlike JAX where we wrote Adam manually, MLX provides a stateful optimizer like PyTorch.

151.text: Train for 1000 steps, one document per step. Each iteration: forward, loss, gradients, Adam update.

152.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update model weights.

153.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After processing all names, loop back for another pass.

154.text: Convert the name into a sequence of integer token IDs with BOS markers at both ends.

155.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this guard prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

157.text: Convert the Python token list to an MLX array so it can flow through the computation graph for forward and backward passes.

158.text: Wrap the target tokens as an MLX array. These are the correct next characters the model should learn to predict.

160.text: Compute loss and gradients in one call. Clean and efficient.

163.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

164.text: Update the optimizer's learning rate. Must wrap in mx.array, MLX is strict about types.

165.text: Push the computed gradients into the model's parameters via the optimizer. MLX's stateful optimizer applies the Adam update rule internally.

166.text: Force evaluation. MLX uses lazy evaluation, operations build a computation graph but don't execute until you call mx.eval. This lets MLX fuse and optimize operations. Without this call, nothing would actually compute.

168.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

169.text: .item() extracts a Python scalar from an MLX array.

174.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

176.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

177.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

178.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens through the full model.

180.text: Run the full model on the current token sequence to predict the next character.

181.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top predictions.

182.text: Sample from categorical distribution. MLX's RNG doesn't require explicit key management like JAX.

183.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

185.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

186.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to produce the generated name as a readable string.

187.text: Print the finished name. These names were invented by the model from statistical patterns it learned during training.
